Smart Lister: Designing an AI System Merchants Could Actually Trust

How I designed an AI-powered listing assistant that cut merchant listing time by around 40% across 10,000+ SKUs — by keeping humans in control of every AI decision.

AI/ML Product Design 0 to 1 Lead Product Designer Threecolts 4 Weeks SaaS · eCommerce

Smart Lister

AI-Powered Listing Optimization

Add project cover image here

My Role
Lead Product Designer
Scope
End-to-end feature design
Timeline
4 weeks
Team
Developers, PMs, Marketing
Domain
AI · Marketplace · SaaS
01 · Overview

What is Smart Lister?

Smart Lister is an AI-powered listing assistant embedded within the CedCommerce and Threecolts marketplace ecosystem. It was designed to help online sellers generate, optimize, and enrich product listings across multiple marketplaces with significantly less manual effort.

The product sits at the intersection of AI utility and merchant tooling. For sellers managing large catalogs across Amazon, eBay, and Shopify, it targets the most time-consuming part of their day: getting products listed correctly, at scale, without sacrificing quality.

Business objective: Reduce the operational cost of listing management for merchants, improve listing quality across the platform, and position Threecolts as a smarter, more assistive marketplace tool compared to competitors.
02 · The Problem

Why listing at scale was breaking sellers

Managing product listings sounds simple until you're dealing with hundreds of SKUs, each requiring different content formats for different marketplaces. Sellers were stuck in a cycle of manual, repetitive work that was both time-consuming and error-prone.

📦

Scale without systems

Sellers handling hundreds of SKUs had no efficient way to manage listing creation in bulk. Every product required individual attention.

🎯

Marketplace fragmentation

Each platform had different title formats, category structures, and attribute requirements. A single product needed multiple versions of the same information.

📉

Quality directly impacts revenue

Poor listing quality meant lower search visibility, fewer conversions, and suppressed listings. The stakes were high and the tools weren't keeping up.

🔧

Tools built for input, not intelligence

Existing tools required sellers to do all the thinking. There was no assistance, no automation, and no way to catch common errors before they cost visibility.

User consequence

Sellers were spending a disproportionate amount of time on listing management rather than growing their business. The cognitive load of managing quality across thousands of products was unsustainable.

03 · Product and Business Context

Why this problem was a strategic priority

For Threecolts, listing quality is not a UX nicety — it is a direct driver of merchant success and platform retention. Sellers who list more products, more accurately, generate more revenue and stay on the platform longer.

The competitive landscape was also shifting. Rival tools were beginning to introduce AI assistance for listing tasks. Solving this problem well represented a genuine opportunity to differentiate and build long-term loyalty with high-volume merchants.

Strategic framing: If we could make listing management 40% faster and meaningfully more accurate, we were not just saving time. We were removing one of the biggest operational barriers stopping merchants from scaling their catalogs.
04 · My Role and Ownership

What I owned on this project

I led the end-to-end product design for Smart Lister, from discovery through to final handoff. This was a 0 to 1 project, which meant there were no existing patterns to follow. Every interaction model, AI disclosure pattern, and workflow decision was defined from scratch.

→

What I owned

Problem framing, user research synthesis, IA, user flows, interaction design, high-fidelity UI, and handoff documentation for all four feature areas.

→

Decisions I drove

AI disclosure model, bulk vs. individual interaction patterns, the trust-first approach to automation, and how AI suggestions were surfaced without overriding user control.

→

Cross-functional work

Worked closely with PMs to define scope and prioritisation, engineering to understand AI capability constraints, and marketing to align on feature positioning.

→

Leadership

Ran design reviews, presented trade-off rationale to stakeholders, and advocated for user control as a non-negotiable design constraint throughout the process.

05 · Research and Discovery

Understanding what sellers actually needed

The research phase focused on three areas: understanding the current listing workflow in detail, identifying where the highest-friction moments occurred, and testing early assumptions about what AI assistance would feel useful versus intrusive.

Key research insight 1

Users did not want fully automated systems. The biggest concern was trust — sellers worried that AI-generated content would be inaccurate, on-brand, or worse, cause marketplace penalties. Control was non-negotiable.

Key research insight 2

The most time-consuming work was not listing creation itself, but the micro-tasks surrounding it: writing descriptions, removing backgrounds, mapping attributes, and fixing category errors. These were the real bottlenecks.

Key research insight 3

Bulk operations were seen as high-value but high-risk. Sellers wanted to process many products at once, but feared making a mistake at scale. The UX challenge was making bulk actions feel safe, not just fast.

These insights directly shaped the design philosophy: AI would assist every step, but humans would always retain the final decision. This was not a small constraint — it informed every interaction in the product.

06 · Defining the Core UX Challenge

The real design problem was trust, not efficiency

The surface-level brief was to make listing faster. But the deeper challenge was more nuanced: how do you design an AI system that sellers would actually rely on, in a context where a wrong AI output could directly damage their marketplace performance?

Core UX challenge: Design a system where AI does the heavy lifting without ever making sellers feel like they have lost control. Every automation needed a visible override. Every suggestion needed to feel earned, not imposed.

This reframing changed how I approached every feature. The question was never just "how does this work?" It was always: "will a seller trust this enough to use it on 500 products at once?"

07 · Strategy and Design Approach

Four principles that guided every decision

1

AI as assistant, not authority

Every AI output was presented as a suggestion, not a final state. Sellers could review, edit, or reject anything the system generated before it went live.

2

Embedded, not bolted on

Smart Lister was integrated directly into the existing listing flow. Switching to a separate AI tool would break context and reduce adoption. The AI had to live where the work happened.

3

Bulk and individual parity

Every feature had to work at both the single-product and bulk level. Designing for bulk meant accounting for progress feedback, error states, and partial failures — none of which were trivial.

4

Always show what changed

After any AI action, the system made it immediately clear what had been modified and what still needed attention. Transparency was the foundation of trust.

08 · Solution Breakdown

Four features, one coherent system

Smart Lister was designed as a multi-layer AI system, each feature targeting a distinct part of the listing workflow. Together they formed a complete solution to the scale problem.

01

Image Background Removal

Product images with inconsistent or cluttered backgrounds reduced visual quality and triggered marketplace compliance failures. Sellers were paying for external tools or spending hours editing manually.

The solution
  • AI-powered background removal at individual and bulk level
  • Works across entire catalog selection
UX rationale
  • Preview before applying any changes
  • Batch processing with visible progress feedback
  • One-click revert for any image
Impact
  • Improved visual consistency at scale
  • Eliminated dependency on external editing tools
Design screens
1Seller selects product image from catalog
→
2AI processes removal with live preview
→
3Result previewed before saving. Seller stays in control
1Select 50+ products and trigger Remove Image Background from bulk actions
→
2Modal shows per-image preview. AI edits 100 images across 10 products simultaneously
→
3Success confirmation. Seller sees exactly what changed before committing
View full design in Figma Opens in a new tab · View only
02

Product Category Suggestion

Incorrect category selection was a silent conversion killer — affecting search visibility and discoverability without any obvious signal to the seller that something was wrong.

The solution
  • AI suggests relevant categories based on product data
  • Ranked suggestions with confidence signals
UX rationale
  • Top suggestions shown, never auto-applied
  • Full manual override always available
  • Confidence indicators help sellers decide quickly
Impact
  • Reduced decision fatigue on category mapping
  • Improved listing accuracy and discoverability
Design screens — Category selection flow
1Category selector opens. AI Assist available alongside manual browse
2AI suggests relevant product types based on product data
3Ranked category hierarchy returned. Seller selects, never forced to accept
4Full category path confirmed with breadcrumb. Seller can clear and restart anytime
View full design in Figma Opens in a new tab · View only
03

AI Product Description Generator

Writing SEO-optimised product descriptions was one of the most time-consuming tasks in the listing workflow, and one of the most inconsistent. Quality varied wildly across a seller's catalog.

The solution
  • AI generates optimised descriptions from basic product inputs
  • Multiple variations offered per product
UX rationale
  • Fully editable output — critical for trust
  • Structured formatting for readability
  • Variation options reduce cognitive lock-in
Impact
  • Faster content creation across large catalogs
  • Consistent listing quality platform-wide
Design screens — AI description trim flow
1Description exceeds Amazon's 2000 char limit. AI Assist prompt surfaces in context
2Modal shows original text. Seller reviews before triggering AI trim
3Skeleton loader while AI processes. Clear feedback that something is happening
4Trimmed result highlighted. Seller applies or discards
View full design in Figma Opens in a new tab · View only
04

Value Mapping

Mapping product attributes to marketplace-specific fields was complex, error-prone, and invisible — sellers rarely knew a mapping was wrong until a listing was rejected or suppressed.

The solution
  • Intelligent mapping between product data and required listing attributes
UX rationale
  • Clear field-to-field mapping visibility
  • Missing or mismatched values highlighted proactively
  • Manual corrections always permitted
Impact
  • Reduced listing errors and rejections
  • Improved compliance with marketplace requirements
Design screens — Value mapping flow
1Mapping table shows Shopify values alongside empty Amazon fields. Seller can map manually
→
2AI Assist triggered. Maps all 56 variants simultaneously with live progress feedback
→
3Amazon recommended values filled in. Seller reviews each field and saves — or overrides individually
View full design in Figma Opens in a new tab · View only
09 · Constraints and Trade-offs

The decisions that shaped the product

AI trust vs. automation speed

The fastest experience would have been full automation. But research made clear that sellers would not trust a system that changed things without their knowledge. Every efficiency gain had to be balanced against the need for review and control. We chose trust over speed, and designed the system accordingly.

Bulk power vs. error risk

Bulk operations were the highest-value feature for high-volume sellers. But a mistake applied to 500 products at once was a serious business risk. We solved this with preview states, progress feedback, and granular revert options — which added complexity to the engineering scope but were non-negotiable from a UX standpoint.

Feature scope within four weeks

The timeline was tight. Prioritisation decisions were driven by user impact: background removal and description generation were the highest-frequency pain points, so they received the most design attention. Value mapping, while critical, was scoped for a more streamlined initial implementation.

Embedded integration vs. standalone tool

There was an early proposal to build Smart Lister as a separate module. I advocated against this, as a separate context would break the listing flow and reduce adoption. Keeping it embedded meant tighter engineering constraints, but significantly better UX.

10 · Outcome and Impact

What changed for merchants

⚡

Around 40% reduction in listing time

Across 10,000+ SKUs, merchants completed listing workflows significantly faster, freeing time for higher-value business tasks.

🎯

Improved SEO and discoverability

AI-generated descriptions and accurate category suggestions improved listing quality, which directly affects search ranking and conversion on marketplace platforms.

📉

Reduced operational complexity

Sellers replaced multiple external tools and manual workarounds with a single, embedded workflow. The cognitive overhead of managing large catalogs dropped substantially.

📈

Scalable catalog management

For the first time, merchants could manage listings at a scale that was previously impractical. The system was designed to work just as well at 50 SKUs as at 5,000.

11 · Learnings and Reflection

What I took away from this project

01

AI transparency is a product feature, not a disclaimer

Every time a user can see what the AI changed and why, it builds the trust that makes automation valuable. Designing for transparency was as important as designing the AI output itself. If I were building this again, I would invest even more in post-action states and audit trails.

02

Small, repetitive tasks are the highest-leverage UX opportunity

The biggest wins were not in the obvious headline features. They were in the micro-tasks that sellers repeated hundreds of times a day. Optimisation and enrichment tools added more value than creation tools, because they addressed volume, not just capability.

03

Bulk operations demand a different design language

Designing for single actions and designing for bulk actions require fundamentally different thinking. Progress, partial failure, and recovery states all need to be first-class design concerns, not afterthoughts. I underestimated this initially and had to revisit the bulk flows mid-project.

04

If I were doing this again

I would push earlier for qualitative feedback sessions with high-volume merchants during the design phase, not just discovery. The insights from users managing 1,000+ SKUs were qualitatively different from those managing 50, and that difference shaped several late-stage design decisions that would have been better made earlier.

The takeaway

Smart Lister transforms product listing from a manual, repetitive task into an intelligent, scalable workflow. The central design insight was this: AI is only as valuable as the trust it earns. Every decision in this project was made in service of that principle.

Mariya Khan · Senior Product Designer · 2025
